x_posts.text: Asking for referrals is the least reliable way to get them. Currency first. Then spend it.
Owners don't share your management pitch. They share the insight you gave them that made them look sharp in front of a neighbor. Those are not the same thing.
The owner who passed on you in January is probably ready now. Most VRM deals aren't lost to a better competitor. They're lost to timing. Top of mind when the frustration peaks is the whole game.

x_posts.source_theme: stories vs data in listing appointments
x_posts.text: Data makes owners skeptical. Stories make them nod. The listing appointment that leads with a revenue projection is doing the hardest kind of persuasion.

The most powerful line in a listing appointment: 'Let me tell you about a client I work with who was in almost exactly your situation when we first talked...' That's the entry point. The rest of the story does the closing.

Curious what's actually working for referrals in VRM right now. Are owners in your market referring unprompted, or does it always require a direct ask? What's driving the ones that come in without prompting?
'Just checking in' is the lowest-value touchpoint you can send. It tells the owner exactly one thing: you haven't forgotten you want their listing.
Went deeper on the referral engine and transition story system in this week's VRM Sales Library newsletter. Three practices that compound over a season instead of asking for favors.

threads.source_theme: three transition stories to have ready
tweets: Most VRM operators tell transition stories reactively. A prospect seems skeptical, you recall a client situation. The story is half-formed because you're building it under pressure.

The operators who use them well have three stories built in advance: the self-managing owner who burned out, the bad-experience switcher who was afraid to try again, and the stable owner who didn't think they had a problem.
Each one is four to six sentences. Situation, decision, outcome, and the one thing the owner said that surprised you.
Write them down. Practice the details until they're clean. The story you tell from memory in a listing appointment is always better than the one you construct on the fly.
